title: 19_monthly_orders.ipynb
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Months in the Right Order

You plotted monthly orders for the year. The bars are the right heights — but the x-axis reads `Apr, Aug, Dec, Feb, Jan, Jul, Jun, Mar, May, Nov, Oct, Sep`. That's alphabetical, not chronological.

You stored the months as **strings**, and your code calls `sorted(orders_by_month.items())`. Sorting strings is *lexical* (alphabetical) — `"Apr" < "Aug" < "Dec"` — so Python obeys the request and you get nonsense for a time series.

Give the sort a hint about chronological order, or use `pd.to_datetime` so dates are real dates.
initialCode:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

orders_by_month = {
    "Jan": 2300, "Feb": 2600, "Mar": 2900, "Apr": 3400,
    "May": 3700, "Jun": 4100, "Jul": 4400, "Aug": 4900,
    "Sep": 5100, "Oct": 5600, "Nov": 6200, "Dec": 6800,
}
rows = sorted(orders_by_month.items())
months, totals = zip(*rows)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.plot(months, totals, marker="o", color="#E8B65A")
ax.set_title("monthly_orders_2025.png")
ax.set_xlabel("month")
ax.set_ylabel("orders")
plt.show()
traceback:
<Figure size 700x400 with 1 Axes>
# x-axis: Apr, Aug, Dec, Feb, Jan, Jul, Jun, Mar, May, Nov, Oct, Sep — alphabetical, not chronological
correctOutput:
<Figure size 700x400 with 1 Axes>
# x-axis: Jan, Feb, Mar, Apr, May, Jun, Jul, Aug, Sep, Oct, Nov, Dec — chronological

options.label: Sort with a chronological key list and `key=` to look up each month's position
options.patchCode:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

MONTH_ORDER = ["Jan","Feb","Mar","Apr","May","Jun","Jul","Aug","Sep","Oct","Nov","Dec"]
orders_by_month = {
    "Jan": 2300, "Feb": 2600, "Mar": 2900, "Apr": 3400,
    "May": 3700, "Jun": 4100, "Jul": 4400, "Aug": 4900,
    "Sep": 5100, "Oct": 5600, "Nov": 6200, "Dec": 6800,
}
rows = sorted(orders_by_month.items(), key=lambda kv: MONTH_ORDER.index(kv[0]))
months, totals = zip(*rows)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.plot(months, totals, marker="o", color="#7CD992")
ax.set_title("monthly_orders_2025.png")
ax.set_xlabel("month")
ax.set_ylabel("orders")
plt.show()
options.resultLog:
<Figure size 700x400 with 1 Axes>
# x-axis: Jan, Feb, Mar, Apr, May, Jun, Jul, Aug, Sep, Oct, Nov, Dec — line climbs across the year
options.rationale: `key=lambda kv: MONTH_ORDER.index(kv[0])` tells `sorted` to **compare by chronological position**, not by the string itself. Each month is mapped to `0..11` and sorted by that integer, so January (0) comes before April (3). This is the standard fix when your data is categorical-with-known-order. For real date columns (`pd.to_datetime`), you'd let pandas do the sorting natively.

options.label: Wrap the months in `pd.to_datetime` for sorting
options.patchCode:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd

orders_by_month = {
    "Jan": 2300, "Feb": 2600, "Mar": 2900, "Apr": 3400,
    "May": 3700, "Jun": 4100, "Jul": 4400, "Aug": 4900,
    "Sep": 5100, "Oct": 5600, "Nov": 6200, "Dec": 6800,
}
rows = sorted(orders_by_month.items(), key=lambda kv: pd.to_datetime(kv[0], format="%b"))
months, totals = zip(*rows)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.plot(months, totals, marker="o", color="#E8B65A")
ax.set_title("monthly_orders_2025.png")
ax.set_xlabel("month")
ax.set_ylabel("orders")
plt.show()
options.resultLog:
<Figure size 700x400 with 1 Axes>
# x-axis correct, but pd.to_datetime called 12 times inside sorted — slow and brittle (locale-dependent)
options.rationale: Sortable result, but `pd.to_datetime("Jan", format="%b")` is **locale-dependent** — on a non-English locale it returns `NaT` and the sort silently uses defaults. Also runs the parse 12 times inside the comparator. A small `MONTH_ORDER` lookup is faster and predictable. (`pd.to_datetime` is the right tool when your source data is real date strings like `"2025-01-15"` — the rule is *use it on real dates, not abbreviations*.)

options.label: Force the x-axis order with `ax.set_xticks(MONTH_ORDER)` after plotting
options.patchCode:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

MONTH_ORDER = ["Jan","Feb","Mar","Apr","May","Jun","Jul","Aug","Sep","Oct","Nov","Dec"]
orders_by_month = {
    "Jan": 2300, "Feb": 2600, "Mar": 2900, "Apr": 3400,
    "May": 3700, "Jun": 4100, "Jul": 4400, "Aug": 4900,
    "Sep": 5100, "Oct": 5600, "Nov": 6200, "Dec": 6800,
}
rows = sorted(orders_by_month.items())
months, totals = zip(*rows)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.plot(months, totals, marker="o", color="#E8B65A")
ax.set_xticks(MONTH_ORDER)
ax.set_title("monthly_orders_2025.png")
ax.set_xlabel("month")
ax.set_ylabel("orders")
plt.show()
options.resultLog:
<Figure size 700x400 with 1 Axes>
# tick labels show chronological order, but the LINE still connects Apr → Aug → Dec → Feb …
options.rationale: This relabels the x-axis ticks but the **line is already drawn** through the points in the (alphabetical) order they were plotted. So the labels say Jan-Feb-Mar but the line zig-zags Apr→Aug→Dec→Feb→Jan→…. Cosmetic patch, structural bug. The order has to be fixed before plotting.

hints: Print `rows` after `sorted(orders_by_month.items())`. The keys are strings, so Python sorts them alphabetically.
Pass `key=lambda kv: MONTH_ORDER.index(kv[0])` so the sort uses chronological position, not the month string itself.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
Python's `sorted()` on strings is **lexical** — character by character, treating `"Apr" < "Aug"` because `'p' < 'u'`. For real date strings like `"2025-01-15"` this accidentally matches chronological order (`YYYY-MM-DD` happens to sort right), but for abbreviated month names you get the alphabet.

A chart drawn after a wrong-order sort doesn't just relabel — the **line connects points in whatever order they appear**, so the visual shape is also nonsense.

### Why the fix is correct
`MONTH_ORDER.index(name)` maps each abbreviation to its chronological position (`Jan` → 0, `Feb` → 1, …, `Dec` → 11). Sorting by that integer puts the months in calendar order. The line then connects points in chronological order, the x-axis labels read left-to-right Jan..Dec, and the picture matches reality.

The broader principle: **if a value has a meaningful order other than its lexical one, the sort must know about that order**. For real date columns, pandas/Polars do this for you (`pd.to_datetime` + `df.sort_values("date")`). For categorical-with-order data (months, weekdays, sizes `S/M/L`), keep an explicit `ORDER` list nearby and sort by `ORDER.index(...)` — or use a `pd.Categorical` with `ordered=True`.
recruiterReview: Solid — you recognised that string sort and chronological sort are different things, and fixed the order at the **data layer** rather than retro-relabelling the axis. That's the pattern that survives later changes (a new month column, a different chart type). Two follow-ups: 1) `pd.Categorical([...], categories=MONTH_ORDER, ordered=True)` makes the order a property of the data, so every subsequent groupby/plot inherits it. 2) For real timestamps, store them as `datetime`/`Timestamp` from the start — string dates are a recurring source of this bug. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## Sorting Time-Like Data

A shockingly large amount of analyst time goes into not letting strings pretend to be dates. The cheapest fix is to never store dates as strings in the first place; the second-cheapest is to know how to sort them correctly when you have to.

### Lexical vs chronological
Python's `sorted()` compares strings character-by-character:
```python
sorted(["Mar", "Jan", "Feb"])   # ['Feb', 'Jan', 'Mar']
sorted(["Apr", "Aug", "Dec"])   # ['Apr', 'Aug', 'Dec']  (alphabetical)
```
For month abbreviations this is wrong. For full ISO dates `"2025-01-15"` it happens to be right (sorting works because `YYYY-MM-DD` is *designed* to sort lexically). For US-formatted strings like `"01/15/2025"` it's wrong again.

### Three fixes, ranked
#### 1) Use real datetime objects (the best fix)
```python
import pandas as pd
df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["date"])
df = df.sort_values("date")
```
Now the order is intrinsic to the data — every subsequent operation (groupby, resample, plot) will respect it.

#### 2) Explicit order list (when categorical-with-order)
```python
MONTH_ORDER = ["Jan","Feb","Mar","Apr","May","Jun",
               "Jul","Aug","Sep","Oct","Nov","Dec"]
sorted(items, key=lambda kv: MONTH_ORDER.index(kv[0]))
```
Fast, predictable, locale-independent. Works for any ordered categorical (weekdays, sizes `S/M/L`, severity `low/med/high`).

#### 3) Pandas Categorical with `ordered=True`
```python
df["month"] = pd.Categorical(df["month"], categories=MONTH_ORDER, ordered=True)
df = df.sort_values("month")
```
Bakes the order into the column. All future sorts/plots inherit it.

### The trap: relabelling the axis after the fact
```python
ax.plot(months, totals)         # connects points in whatever order `months` is in
ax.set_xticks(CORRECT_ORDER)    # only changes the labels, not the line
```
If the line is drawn through an out-of-order sequence, relabelling the axis just makes the labels and the line disagree. **Fix the order before plotting.**

### Locales — a hidden trap
`pd.to_datetime("Janv", format="%b")` works on a French-locale machine but fails on English. `datetime.strptime("Jan", "%b")` is similarly locale-sensitive. The cheapest portable fix is an explicit order list. If you must parse abbreviations, set the locale explicitly with `locale.setlocale(...)` so the behaviour is reproducible.

### Common beginner mistakes
- Storing dates as strings and being surprised by lexical sort.
- Trusting `sorted(["01/15/2024", "02/03/2024"])` because two examples happen to be in order.
- Calling `set_xticks` after the line is drawn (cosmetic patch).

### You just learned…
String sort and chronological sort aren't the same. Use real `datetime` types when you can; use an explicit order list when you can't; never rely on retroactive axis relabelling to fix order bugs.

videos.title: Why my month chart is in alphabetical order
videos.searchQuery: matplotlib months alphabetical order chronological fix

conceptCard:
This mission is about **chronological vs lexical order**.

- `sorted()` on strings is **alphabetical** — `"Apr" < "Aug" < "Dec"`.
- A line plot connects points in the order it sees them — wrong order = zig-zag.
- Use real `datetime` types, an explicit order list, or `pd.Categorical(ordered=True)`.

The code sorts month strings lexically, so the line wanders. Sort with `key=lambda kv: MONTH_ORDER.index(kv[0])`.

lineNotes.noteMd: The dict — order doesn't matter here, but the keys are 3-letter abbreviations.
**The bug.** `sorted(items)` on a dict of string keys uses lexical order. Pass `key=` with a chronological index.
`zip(*rows)` splits `[("Jan", 2300), …]` into two parallel tuples for plotting.
`plot()` draws the line in `months` order — whatever order `sorted` produced.

takeaway: String sort is lexical, not chronological — use a key= mapping or real datetime types.
